title: Topic 131: The Psychology of Game Passes: Paying for Access vs. Ownership
content:
### Abstract

This paper delves into the burgeoning phenomenon of video game subscription services, colloquially known as "game passes." We explore the psychological underpinnings that make these services so alluring, and the curious consumer behaviors they engender. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of existing literature and real-world examples, we dissect the cognitive biases and emotional triggers that game passes exploit, from the paralyzing "paradox of choice" to the insidious "sunk cost fallacy." We argue that while game passes offer the illusion of infinite gaming bliss, they may be subtly rewiring our brains to favor fleeting access over the tangible satisfaction of ownership. This paper is a must-read for anyone who has ever subscribed to a game pass, and for anyone who wants to understand the future of digital consumption, one monthly payment at a time.

### Introduction

It's a familiar scene in the modern gamer's life: you sit down, controller in hand, ready to conquer virtual worlds. But instead of booting up a game, you find yourself scrolling endlessly through a digital library of hundreds, if not thousands, of titles. This is the promise, and the peril, of the game pass. For a seemingly modest monthly fee, services like Xbox Game Pass and PlayStation Plus offer a smorgasbord of digital delights, a veritable all-you-can-eat buffet for the gaming soul. But as with any buffet, there's a catch. The sheer abundance of choice can be overwhelming, leading to a state of decision paralysis that would make a existentialist philosopher proud. You spend more time choosing a game than playing one, and when you finally do make a choice, you're haunted by the ghosts of all the other games you could be playing.

This paper examines the psychological warfare being waged for our entertainment dollars. We will explore the subtle, and not-so-subtle, ways in which game passes manipulate our cognitive biases to keep us subscribed. We will delve into the psychology of ownership, and how the shift from physical media to digital access is changing our relationship with the games we play. Are we truly getting a better deal, or are we simply trading the satisfaction of ownership for the fleeting illusion of choice? Join us as we journey down the rabbit hole of game passes, and emerge with a deeper understanding of the forces that shape our digital lives. 

### Literature Review

The academic world has, in its infinite wisdom, begun to turn its discerning eye to the world of video games, and the results are as fascinating as they are, at times, unintentionally hilarious. A growing body of research is exploring the psychological impact of the subscription model, and the picture it paints is one of a carefully crafted ecosystem designed to keep us hooked. One study, with the rather dry title of "The rise of the subscription model in the video game console industry: Unveiling the commercial consequences for platform owners and video game sellers," found that game passes, far from cannibalizing sales, actually *enhance* console revenue and lead to a "healthier video game supply" [1]. In other words, we're not just playing more games, we're playing *better* games, or at least more of them. The study also notes that, unlike their counterparts in the music and movie industries, video game subscription services still prominently feature an ownership-based purchase model, allowing players to buy games for keeps. This, the authors suggest, creates a more harmonious relationship between access and ownership, a sort of digital détente.

But not all is rosy in the land of subscription services. Another study, "The Psychology of Subscription Models: Why Consumers Stay Hooked on Monthly Payments," delves into the darker side of our subscription obsession [2]. The authors identify a rogues' gallery of psychological biases that keep us paying, month after month, for services we may not even be using. There's "inertia," our tendency to stick with the default option, and the "sunk cost fallacy," that nagging feeling that we need to keep playing a game we're not enjoying just because we've already invested so much time in it. And let's not forget "loss aversion," our deep-seated fear of losing access to all those games we've downloaded but never played. The study also points a finger at the companies themselves, who employ a variety of tactics, from auto-renewals to byzantine cancellation procedures, to keep us on the hook. It's a sobering reminder that, in the world of subscription services, the house always wins.

Finally, we have the thorny issue of psychological ownership. A study titled "Exploring the effects of psychological ownership, gaming motivations, and primary/secondary control on online game addiction" found that our desire for achievement and escapism is a strong predictor of our feelings of ownership over our virtual worlds [3]. But here's the twist: the study also found an inverted-U relationship between psychological ownership and online game addiction. Too little ownership, and we're not invested enough to get addicted. Too much, and we're so enmeshed in our virtual lives that we can't see the forest for the procedurally generated trees. It's a delicate balancing act, and one that game developers are still struggling to understand.

### Analysis

So, what does this all mean for the average gamer? It means that we are all unwitting participants in a grand psychological experiment. Game passes are not just a new way to buy games; they are a new way of thinking about games, and about our relationship with them. The shift from ownership to access is a profound one, with far-reaching implications for our consumer habits and our mental well-being.

At the heart of the game pass phenomenon is the **paradox of choice**. In theory, more choice is always better. But in practice, an overabundance of choice can be paralyzing. When faced with a library of hundreds of games, we are often unable to make a decision. We scroll and scroll, our thumbs getting a better workout than our brains, until we finally give up and watch a movie instead. This is not a sign of weakness; it is a well-documented psychological phenomenon. The more choices we have, the more likely we are to feel regret about the choices we make, and the less satisfied we are with our final decision.

Then there is the issue of **devalued ownership**. When we own a game, we have a tangible connection to it. We have the box, the disc, the manual. We have a sense of permanence, of possession. But with a game pass, our ownership is conditional. We have access to the game, but only as long as we keep paying our subscription fee. This can lead to a feeling of detachment, of disposability. We are less likely to invest ourselves in a game that we know could disappear from our library at any moment. We become digital tourists, flitting from one game to another, never staying long enough to put down roots.

And let's not forget the **endowment effect**, a cognitive bias that causes us to overvalue things that we own. When we buy a game, we are making a commitment. We are saying, "This game is worth my time and money." This commitment, in turn, makes us more likely to enjoy the game, and to stick with it through the tough times. But with a game pass, there is no commitment. We can try a game for a few minutes and then move on, with no sense of loss. This can lead to a culture of digital promiscuity, where we are always chasing the next new thing, never satisfied with what we have.

Finally, there is the **gamification of consumption**. Game passes are not just about playing games; they are about the meta-game of the game pass itself. We are constantly being nudged and prodded to engage with the service, to try new games, to unlock achievements. We are being conditioned to become a certain type of consumer, one who is always on the lookout for the next new thing, the next dopamine hit. It is a subtle but powerful form of social engineering, and it is changing the way we think about entertainment.

### Real-World Examples

Let us now turn from the abstract to the absurd, from the theoretical to the tragically comical. The real world is rife with examples of the psychological phenomena we have discussed, and they are often funnier, and sadder, than anything we could make up.

Take the **paradox of choice**, for instance. We have all been there. You have a free evening, a rare and precious commodity in this modern age. You could be doing anything. You could be learning a new language, or writing a novel, or finally calling your mother. But instead, you are sitting on your couch, staring at the Xbox Game Pass home screen, your mind a swirling vortex of indecision. "Should I play that critically acclaimed indie game that everyone is talking about?" you wonder. "Or should I finally start that 100-hour RPG that I know I will never finish?" An hour later, you are still scrolling, your thumb numb, your spirit broken. You have become a victim of what we in the academic community call "analysis paralysis," a condition that is becoming increasingly common in the age of infinite choice. In the end, you give up and watch a rerun of a sitcom you've already seen a dozen times. At least there, the choices are made for you.

Then there is the cautionary tale of **devalued ownership**. Remember *Scott Pilgrim vs. The World: The Game*? It was a delightful retro-style beat-'em-up, a loving homage to the classic games of yesteryear. It was also a digital-only release, and when the licensing agreements expired, it vanished from digital storefronts, like a digital ghost. For years, the only way to play it was to have already purchased it, a small consolation for those who had. The game was eventually re-released, but the incident served as a stark reminder that, in the digital world, ownership is a fragile thing. We are all just one licensing dispute away from losing our entire digital library. It is a sobering thought, and one that should give us pause the next time we are tempted by the siren song of the game pass.

And what of the **endowment effect**? Consider the case of the full-price game. You have just spent $70 on the latest blockbuster title. You have read the reviews, you have watched the trailers, you are invested. You are going to get your money's worth, even if it kills you. You will slog through the boring tutorial, you will forgive the clunky controls, you will ignore the glaring plot holes. You have made a commitment, and you are going to see it through. Now, contrast that with the game pass game. You have downloaded it for free (or, more accurately, for the price of your monthly subscription). You have no skin in the game, no emotional investment. You play it for ten minutes, you get bored, you delete it. No harm, no foul. But you have also missed out on the potential for a deeper, more meaningful experience. You have traded the satisfaction of a long-term relationship for the fleeting thrill of a one-night stand.

Finally, we have the **gamification of consumption**, as exemplified by the Microsoft Rewards program. Here, the act of choosing a game has been turned into a game itself. You are given a list of tasks to complete: play this game, unlock this achievement, watch this trailer. For each task you complete, you are rewarded with points, which can be redeemed for gift cards, or, more insidiously, for more Game Pass. It is a brilliant, and slightly evil, feedback loop. You are being rewarded for being a good little consumer, for engaging with the ecosystem in the way that Microsoft wants you to. It is a far cry from the simple joy of playing a game for its own sake. It is a sign that we are no longer just players; we are cogs in a much larger machine.

### Discussion

The rise of the game pass is more than just a new business model; it is a cultural phenomenon, a reflection of our changing relationship with technology, with entertainment, and with ourselves. The trends we have observed in the world of video games are not unique to that domain; they are part of a broader shift in our consumer habits, a move away from ownership and towards access. We are becoming a society of subscribers, renting our movies, our music, our software, and even our cars. This has profound implications for our sense of self, for our understanding of value, and for the future of our economy.

What does it mean to own something in the digital age? Is it enough to have access to a file on a server, a file that could be deleted or altered at any time? Or is there something more to ownership, something more tangible, more permanent? The debate over digital rights management (DRM) has been raging for years, and the game pass has only added fuel to the fire. Are we, as consumers, willing to trade the security of ownership for the convenience of access? The answer, it seems, is a resounding "yes." We have been seduced by the siren song of the subscription, and we are not looking back.

But at what cost? The psychological biases that we have discussed in this paper – the paradox of choice, the endowment effect, the sunk cost fallacy – are not just academic curiosities. They are powerful forces that are shaping our behavior, often without our knowledge or consent. We are being nudged and prodded, manipulated and monetized, all in the name of a seamless user experience. It is a Faustian bargain, and we are all too eager to sign on the dotted line.

This is not to say that game passes are inherently evil. They offer a great deal of value to consumers, and they have opened up the world of gaming to a whole new audience. But we must be aware of the psychological forces at play. We must be mindful of the trade-offs we are making, and we must be prepared to live with the consequences. The future of entertainment is not a spectator sport; it is an interactive one. And we are all players in the game.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, the game pass is a double-edged sword, a digital chalice that is both half full and half empty. It offers us a world of infinite gaming, a cornucopia of digital delights, but it also threatens to turn us into a generation of digital dilettantes, flitting from one game to another, never truly committing, never truly owning. We have explored the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, from the paralyzing paradox of choice to the insidious endowment effect, and we have seen how these forces are shaping our behavior in ways that we are only just beginning to understand.

So, what is the takeaway from all of this? Should we cancel our subscriptions, delete our accounts, and return to the good old days of physical media? Of course not. That would be like trying to put the digital genie back in the bottle. The game pass is here to stay, and we must learn to live with it. But we can do so with our eyes open. We can be mindful of the psychological forces at play, and we can make a conscious effort to resist their siren song. We can choose to own our games, both literally and figuratively. We can choose to be players, not just consumers. The choice, as always, is ours.
references:
[1] Van Crombrugge, M., Stremersch, S., & Verniers, I. (2025). The rise of the subscription model in the video game console industry: Unveiling the commercial consequences for platform owners and video game sellers. *International Journal of Research in Marketing*, *42*(4), 1058-1083. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijresmar.2025.02.006

[2] Chakraborty, S. (2025). The Psychology of Subscription Models: Why Consumers Stay Hooked on Monthly Payments. *International Journal of Research and Innovation*, *10*(5), 2456-3315.

[3] Wang, X., Abdelhamid, M., & Sanders, G. L. (2021). Exploring the effects of psychological ownership, gaming motivations, and primary/secondary control on online game addiction. *Decision Support Systems*, *144*, 113512. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dss.2021.113512
